one_line_takeaway: ET3 minimizes a JEM-style energy (negative LogSumExp of logits) via a few gradient steps at test time to boost adversarial robustness of CLIP zero-shot classifiers and, by extension, LVLMs like LLaVA, backed by a formal (binary-classifier) correctness proof and validated with double-digit robust-accuracy gains including under BPDA-based adaptive attacks.
key_finding: ET3 improves robust accuracy consistently and with low overhead (as little as 2.3% extra inference time in the single-step LVLM setting). Representative numbers taken directly from the paper's own tables: on ViT-L/14 TeCoA (eps_t=4/255) zero-shot classification across 14 datasets under AutoAttack (eps_a=4/255), average robust accuracy rises from 32.85% (base) to 40.93% with ET3 (+8.08 pts); for FARE (eps_t=2/255) the gain is largest, from 20.39% to 31.75% (+11.36 pts). Against dedicated test-time defenses on 8 fine-grained datasets (TeCoA CLIP-ViT-B/32, PGD-100), ET3 alone reaches 16.51% average robust accuracy vs. 12.26% for the undefended robust CLIP and 13.90% for TTC (+4.25 pts over TTC); combined with R-TPT it reaches 23.88% vs. R-TPT alone's 21.41% (+11.62 pts over the undefended baseline). On LLaVA 1.5-7B (standard CLIP encoder, eps_a=4/255), average adversarial score across COCO/Flickr30k/TextVQA/VQAv2 improves from 1.0 to 38.8 (+37.8) with ET3, while clean performance changes only slightly (76.2 -> 73.9, -2.3). Under the defense-aware/adaptive setting on RobustBench ResNet-50, ET3 achieves 37.70% worst-case robust accuracy under targeted APGD + BPDA, versus 34.96% for the undefended base model, 34.68% for Singh et al. (-0.28 vs. base), and 35.48% for Kulkarni et al./IW-WC (+0.52 vs base) -- i.e., ET3's +2.74-point gain over base is larger than the other two TTT baselines evaluated on the same adaptive attack. Diffusion-based purification (DiffPure, GDMP) is shown, consistent with prior work (DiffBreak), to collapse much further under adaptive attack (12.11%-25.00%) despite similar clean accuracy.
core_contribution: The paper proposes ET3 (Energy-Guided Test-Time Transformation), a training-free, inference-time defense that boosts adversarial robustness of CLIP-based vision encoders and downstream Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). ET3 treats the classifier's logits as an energy function E(x) = -LogSumExp(f_theta(x)) (the JEM view of a softmax classifier) and, at test time, takes T gradient steps on the input, projected onto an L2 (or L_inf) epsilon-ball, to minimize this energy: x_tilde = x + z, z obtained via projected gradient descent on E(.). The transformed image/embedding is then fed to the (unmodified, frozen) classifier, CLIP zero-shot head, or LLaVA's vision encoder -> projection layer -> LLM pipeline. No auxiliary generative model, retraining, or prompt optimization is required; a single or two-step optimization through the vision encoder alone is enough to improve downstream captioning/VQA robustness, since the VLM itself is never touched.
threat_model: Two threat models are evaluated: (1) a defense-unaware/static-attack setting, where the adversary (AutoAttack: APGD-CE + APGD-DLR at 100 iterations, or PGD-100) crafts perturbations against the base classifier/CLIP encoder without knowledge that ET3 will be applied afterward, at Linf budgets epsilon_a = 4/255 (also swept 2/255-10/255); and (2) a defense-aware/adaptive setting, where the attacker has full knowledge of the ET3 defense and back-propagates through the entire test-time optimization using Backward Pass Differentiable Approximation (BPDA) combined with targeted APGD-DLR, following the adaptive-defense evaluation protocol of Croce et al. (ICML 2022). The adaptive evaluation is restricted to ResNet-50 classifiers on ImageNet (RobustBench) and to LLaVA 1.5-7B, due to the computational cost of BPDA-based adaptive attacks; the paper also reports transfer attacks from the static base model and takes the worst-case accuracy across attacks.
limitations: The formal robustness guarantee (Theorem 4.1) is proved only for binary, two-logit classifiers under a local-linearity assumption and a gradient-norm-ratio condition (C) between ground-truth and incorrect-class gradients; the paper states these assumptions are theoretically verified only for specific constructed robust networks (a two-layer ReLU network on clustered data, and polynomial-ReLU networks from cited prior work), not for the large pretrained CLIP/ViT/LLaVA models actually used in the experiments -- the connection there is only empirically illustrated (Fig. 3, right) via a scatter plot on ImageNet, not proven. Some clean-accuracy costs appear in the tables (e.g., TeCoA eps_t=2/255 clean accuracy drops slightly, -0.17 and -0.15 pts, for two CLIP variants; COCO CIDEr under standard CLIP drops from 115.5 to 111.3 clean). Adaptive-attack (BPDA) evaluation is restricted to ResNet-50 classifiers and LLaVA due to computational cost, so the strongest, most rigorous threat-model results are not obtained for the full range of CLIP backbones/datasets used in the defense-unaware experiments. The authors themselves note as future work that the network would ideally be optimized/trained to better satisfy the local-linearity and gradient-ratio conditions that underlie the theoretical guarantee.

quantitative_results: Zero-shot CLIP ViT-L/14 TeCoA (eps_t=4/255), avg robust accuracy over 14 datasets under AutoAttack eps_a=4/255: base 32.85% -> +ET3 40.93% (+8.08)
Zero-shot CLIP ViT-L/14 FARE (eps_t=2/255), avg robust accuracy: base 20.39% -> +ET3 31.75% (+11.36)

8-dataset fine-grained scope, TeCoA CLIP-ViT-B/32, PGD-100 (eps=4/255): CLIP(Robust) avg robust 12.26%, TTC 13.90%, ET3 16.51% (+4.25 over TTC baseline reported), Ensemble+ET3 22.26%, R-TPT+ET3 23.88% (best overall)
LLaVA 1.5-7B standard CLIP, average adversarial score (eps_a=4/255) across COCO/Flickr30k/TextVQA/VQAv2: 1.0 -> 38.8 with ET3 (+37.8); average clean score 76.2 -> 73.9 (-2.3)
LLaVA 1.5-7B TeCoA2 encoder, average adversarial score: 19.4 -> 35.5 (+16.1); FARE2: 20.4 -> 33.1 (+12.7)

Defense-aware adaptive attack (targeted APGD-DLR + BPDA) on RobustBench ResNet-50: base model 34.96% robust, +Singh et al. 34.68% (-0.28), +Kulkarni et al./IW-WC 35.48% (+0.52), +ET3 37.70% (+2.74) -- ET3 highest among TTT defenses tested
Diffusion-based purification under DiffBreak adaptive attack (for comparison, different base architectures): DiffPure (WRN-50-2) 12.11% robust, DiffPure (DeiT-S) 25.00%, GDMP (DeiT-S) 20.70%
ET3 inference overhead: as little as 2.3% increase in inference time for single-step LVLM defense
baselines_compared: TeCoA (robust CLIP fine-tuning, Mao et al.)
FARE (robust CLIP fine-tuning, Schlarmann et al./cited [72])
Kulkarni et al. / IW-WC interpretability-guided test-time defense ([40])
AutoAttack / APGD-CE / APGD-DLR / PGD-100 (attack methods, not defenses, used to evaluate all of the above)
BPDA (Backward Pass Differentiable Approximation) adaptive-attack methodology of Croce et al. [19]
